briefing:
### Headline

Yesterday, May 19, was a strong traffic day -- sessions hit 37, up 85% versus the 28-day median of 20, and up 60.9% compared to the same day last year (23 sessions). Pageviews reached 142, up 90.6% over the rolling median and up 35.2% YoY, with 28 of 30 users arriving as first-time visitors. This is a genuine positive spike, not a statistical blip, and the YoY confirmation matters: last May 19 was not a strong baseline that would artificially inflate the comparison.

### What happened, and why

The channel story is straightforward: Direct traffic drove nearly all of the gain. The unpacked drill-down shows Direct rose from a baseline of roughly 9.5 sessions to 26, accounting for 16.5 of the ~17 sessions above the median. That means Organic, Referral, and other channels were essentially flat -- this was not a broad search-driven lift. A Direct spike of this size typically points to one of three things: a direct email or newsletter send to a known list, an internal share (staff or close contacts circulating a link), or a branded mention somewhere that resulted in direct URL entry. The data alone does not confirm which -- check with Camy to see whether any email went out on May 19.

Engagement quality supports the idea that these were intentional, motivated visitors rather than accidental arrivals. The engagement rate was 0.9459 (essentially 95% of sessions engaged), average session duration was 143 seconds versus a 28-day median of 115 seconds, and pages-per-session implied by 142 pageviews across 37 sessions is roughly 3.8. That is above-average depth. GSC shows zero impressions and zero clicks for the day, which rules out an organic search surge entirely -- this traffic did not come from Google. That alignment between GA4 (strong) and GSC (flat) is consistent with a Direct/referral event, not an SEO movement.

The recent git commits are all internal operational entries -- daily pulse records and the internal dashboard shipped May 18 -- and none of them correspond to a public-facing page change that would explain a traffic event. Timing does not implicate any content launch.

### Efficacy update

The 30-day launch window shows 11 pages launched and 2 pages modified, with 8 URLs currently ranking in the top 3 and 8 in the top 10 -- meaning every page that has broken into the top 10 is already in the top 3, which is a tight, high-quality cluster. The 49 GA4 sessions attributed to launched content over 30 days is a modest number in absolute terms, but for a specialty counseling center in a single metro market, it is directionally healthy. GSC-attributed impressions and clicks are both recorded as zero in the rollup, which is almost certainly a data-attribution gap rather than a true zero -- GSC data lags by 2-3 days and the rollup window may not yet reflect recent indexing activity for pages launched in the last two weeks.

The top_launches list in the payload is currently dominated entirely by internal git commits with no public URLs attached, so there is no per-page SERP or session data to evaluate at the individual launch level this cycle. The meaningful content launches that produced those 8 top-3 rankings are not surfaced in detail here, which makes it harder to answer Todd's "is it working?" question with specifics. What the data does support: the ranking distribution is good, the attributed sessions are real, and the engagement quality on yesterday's spike suggests that people who find the site are reading it carefully. The backlink profile shows 4 referring domains, 12 total inbound links, and 5 .edu links -- the .edu count is notable for a small local practice and worth tracking.

### Today's actions

1. Check with Camy (or review the email platform -- Mailchimp, Klaviyo, or whatever BCC uses) to see whether an email or newsletter went out on May 19. If it did, note the send size and link destination so you can tie the Direct spike to a source and report it as a confirmed channel event to Todd, not just an unexplained anomaly.

2. Pull the full list of the 8 pages currently ranking in the top 3 from GSC directly (filter by position 1-3, last 28 days). The efficacy rollup confirms they exist but doesn't name them -- having that list in hand gives Todd a concrete answer to "what are we ranking for?" without needing to pull it live in the meeting.

3. Confirm that the internal operational dashboard shipped May 18 is not publicly indexed. Since that commit introduced a new URL structure, do a quick `site:` search or GSC URL inspection to make sure it is either excluded via robots.txt or returning a noindex tag. It should not be ranking.

4. Note the GSC zero-impression / zero-click read for May 19 in your meeting prep as a known data lag, not a signal of organic decline -- so Todd does not interpret the flat GSC numbers as contradicting the strong GA4 day.
